one_line_takeaway: ZO-SPSA shows that fully gradient-free, query-only black-box optimization can jailbreak safety-aligned LVLMs (up to 83.0% ASR on InstructBLIP, human-evaluated) at accuracy comparable to or exceeding white-box gradient attacks under large/unconstrained perturbation budgets, at the cost of roughly 10x more iterations and much longer wall-clock attack time.
key_finding: On AdvBench (human-evaluated, unconstrained perturbation), ZO-SPSA achieves ASR of 83.0% on InstructBLIP, 73.0% on LLaVA, and 60.0% on MiniGPT-4 (Table 3), with per-example attack execution times of 0.91s (InstructBLIP), 6.35s (LLaVA), and 55.03s (MiniGPT-4), and total optimization times of 10.3h/19.4h/29.5h respectively. On the GPT-4o-scored VAJA test set (Table 1), InstructBLIP's Identity-Attack ASR rises from 5.0% (benign image) to 80.0% (unconstrained adversarial image, +75.0 pts); MiniGPT-4 reaches 100% ASR on Disinfo at epsilon=64/255 versus 84.4% for the white-box VAJA baseline at the same setting (Table 2), and ZO-SPSA beats VAJA by +14.6 pts (Identity Attack) and +15.6 pts (Disinformation) at epsilon=64/255, though it underperforms VAJA at smaller budgets. On RealToxicityPrompts (Table 4), ZO-SPSA's unconstrained attack raises InstructBLIP's 'Any' Perspective-API toxicity from 34.2% (clean) to 64.0% and LLaVA's from 9.2% to 60.1%. Transferability (Table 5): adversarial images crafted on MiniGPT-4 as surrogate reach 64.18% toxicity rate on InstructBLIP under Perspective API (vs VAJA's 57.5% at the same transfer setting). Memory/time comparison (Table 6) shows ZO-SPSA uses less GPU memory than white-box VAJA (e.g., MiniGPT-4: 16GB vs 32GB) but far more wall-clock time (e.g., MiniGPT-4: 55h vs 9h), and needs 50,000 iterations versus VAJA's 5,000 to reach comparable effectiveness. Ablations show non-monotonic sensitivity to iteration count under both Gaussian (Table 7) and Rademacher (Table 8) perturbations, e.g., Rademacher 'Any' toxicity peaking at 49.7% at 30,000 iterations versus 42.2%/35.2% at 5,000/40,000 iterations, indicating no simple more-iterations-is-better trend.
core_contribution: The paper proposes ZO-SPSA, a black-box jailbreak attack on Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that crafts adversarial images using Zeroth-Order optimization with Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation. Unlike prior white-box attacks (e.g., the Visual Adversarial Jailbreak Attack, VAJA, by Qi et al. 2024, which the authors treat strictly as a baseline/related work, not their own contribution), ZO-SPSA estimates the gradient of the attack objective purely from input-output query pairs (two forward passes per estimate via a central-difference scheme), requiring no model parameters, no backpropagation, and no surrogate model. The estimated gradient is combined with a sign operation and a PGD-style projected update (Algorithm 1) to iteratively perturb an image so that, paired with a harmful text prompt, the target LVLM is driven to produce harmful/toxic content that a benign image would not elicit. The paper's own experiments (not cited work) evaluate ZO-SPSA on three open-source LVLMs -- InstructBLIP, LLaVA, and MiniGPT-4 -- and additionally study transferability, memory/time cost, and ablations of two hyperparameters (finite-difference perturbation magnitude and Rademacher- vs Gaussian-distributed perturbation directions).
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only input-output query access to a deployed LVLM (no model weights, no gradients, no surrogate model). The attacker optimizes an adversarial image xadv, given a fixed harmful text prompt xt, to maximize the probability the victim model emits harmful responses drawn from a small harmful-response corpus, within a single-turn conversation, and bounded by an L-infinity perturbation budget B (tested at epsilon = 16/255, 32/255, 64/255, and an unconstrained setting). The goal is jailbreaking safety-aligned LVLMs (bypassing alignment/RLHF-based safety mechanisms) via the visual modality rather than text.
limitations: The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) the method requires extensive forward propagation of the target model, causing computational inefficiency, and (2) the optimization process is time-consuming because it relies on noisy gradient approximations. Beyond the authors' own statements: loss trajectories are shown to be non-convergent/oscillatory (Figure 2) rather than monotonically decreasing, so the method lacks convergence guarantees and its stopping point is heuristic; ZO-SPSA needs 10x more iterations than the white-box VAJA baseline to reach comparable ASR and is substantially slower in wall-clock time despite lower memory use; and it underperforms the white-box baseline at smaller perturbation budgets, only matching or exceeding it at larger/unconstrained budgets, which is a less realistic/stealthy threat setting.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) — harmful-behavior instructions, human-evaluated, 66 training + 100 test samples
VAJA test set (Qi et al., 2024) — 40 curated harmful instructions across 4 categories: Identity Attacks, Disinformation, Violence/Crime, Malicious Behaviors toward Humanity (X-risk)
RealToxicityPrompts (Gehman et al., 2020) — 1,225-sample challenging subset, scored via Perspective API and Detoxify across six toxicity attributes
quantitative_results: AdvBench manual-eval ASR: InstructBLIP 83.0%, LLaVA 73.0%, MiniGPT-4 60.0% (Table 3)
AdvBench optimization/execution time: InstructBLIP 10.3h/0.91s, LLaVA 19.4h/6.35s, MiniGPT-4 29.5h/55.03s (Table 3)
GPT-4o-scored VAJA ASR, InstructBLIP Identity Attack: 5.0% (benign) -> 80.0% (unconstrained adversarial), +75.0 pts (Table 1)
MiniGPT-4 Disinfo ASR at epsilon=64/255: 100.0% vs VAJA white-box baseline 84.4% (Table 2)
ZO-SPSA vs VAJA at epsilon=64/255: +14.6 pts Identity Attack, +15.6 pts Disinformation (Table 2)
RealToxicityPrompts 'Any' Perspective API toxicity, InstructBLIP: 34.2% clean -> 64.0% unconstrained; LLaVA: 9.2% clean -> 60.1% unconstrained (Table 4)
Transferability: MiniGPT-4-crafted adversarial images reach 64.18% toxicity rate on InstructBLIP (Perspective API) vs VAJA's 57.5% (Table 5)
GPU memory/time, white-box VAJA vs black-box ZO-SPSA (MiniGPT-4): 32GB/9h vs 16GB/55h (Table 6)
ZO-SPSA requires 50,000 iterations vs VAJA's 5,000 to reach comparable effectiveness
Ablation (Rademacher perturbation), Perspective API 'Any' toxicity: 42.2% at 5K iters, peak 49.7% at 30K iters, 35.2% at 40K iters (Table 8)
baselines_compared: VAJA — Visual Adversarial Jailbreak Attack (Qi et al., 2024), white-box gradient-based jailbreak attack used as the primary comparison baseline throughout (Tables 2, 4, 5, 6)
Benign/clean image (no attack) baseline across all ASR and toxicity tables
